title: French Omelette
description: A pale, silky French omelette — soft and barely set at the centre, rolled into a tender cigar shape with no browned edges. The whole thing cooks in under a minute and demands your full attention while it does.

steps.text: Crack the eggs into a bowl and whisk vigorously with a pinch of salt until the whites and yolks are fully combined — the mixture should be slightly foamy. Do not add milk or water; the French omelette relies solely on egg for its silky texture.

Set a 20 cm non-stick frying pan over high heat. Add the butter and swirl until it melts and the foam subsides — the butter should be hot but not yet browning. If it turns golden, reduce the heat slightly for the next attempt.

Pour in all the beaten egg at once. Immediately begin shaking the pan back and forth while stirring with a heatproof spatula in small rapid circles. The egg will cook in 30–45 seconds and should look softly scrambled and glossy, not dry.

The moment the egg is just set but still glistens on top, stop stirring. Tilt the pan away from you and use the spatula to fold one third of the omelette over the centre, then tip the pan further so the omelette rolls onto the plate seam-side down.

The finished omelette should be a pale ivory-gold on the outside and custardy within, shaped like a small log. Season with a grind of pepper and scatter parsley on top. Serve immediately — a French omelette does not wait.

substitutions.substitute: ghee — gives a similar richness with a faintly nutty aroma
substitutions.notes: use the same quantity

substitutions.substitute: silken tofu (100 g) blended smooth — a vegan variation with a different texture
substitutions.notes: cook on medium heat rather than high

substitutions.substitute: spring-onion greens or fresh coriander leaves
substitutions.notes: both work as garnish

culturalNote: The French omelette is considered a cook's calling card — culinary schools in France famously use it to test whether a student understands heat control and restraint. The goal is precision, not colour.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A good-quality non-stick tawa (24–26 cm) works perfectly in place of the traditional omelette pan. Indian gas burners run hotter than European electric hobs, so keep the flame medium-high rather than full blast and be ready to move fast. Amul unsalted butter performs exactly as needed here.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Soft-Scrambled Eggs on Buttered Toast
description: Creamy, gently stirred scrambled eggs cooked over low heat until just barely set, piled high on thick buttered toast. This is the British breakfast at its most comforting and most forgiving.

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.notes: divided — 1 tbsp for eggs, 1 tbsp to spread on toast

ingredients.name: fresh cream
ingredients.notes: adds extra lusciousness; skip for a lighter result

ingredients.name: spring onion greens, sliced
ingredients.notes: for garnish

steps.text: Toast the bread in a pan or under a grill until golden on both sides. Spread one tablespoon of butter generously over each slice while still hot, then set aside on warmed plates.

Crack the eggs into a bowl, add the cream if using, season with salt and pepper, and beat gently with a fork until just combined — you want streaks of yolk visible, not a uniform mixture. Over-beating makes the eggs tough.

Place the remaining butter in a small non-stick saucepan or frying pan over the lowest possible flame. Pour in the egg mixture and begin stirring slowly with a wooden spoon or silicone spatula, moving it across the entire base of the pan in lazy figure-of-eight strokes.

Every 20–30 seconds lift the pan off the heat entirely for a few seconds while continuing to stir — this prevents the eggs from overcooking. The whole process takes 4–5 minutes and the eggs should finish in large, glossy, barely-set curds that hold their shape.

Remove from the heat while the eggs still look slightly underdone — residual heat will finish the job as you plate. Spoon immediately over the buttered toast, scatter spring onion greens on top and serve at once.

substitutions.substitute: milk or a teaspoon of curd stirred in at the end
substitutions.notes: milk gives a lighter result; curd adds a gentle tang

substitutions.substitute: ghee — richer flavour and a higher smoke point
substitutions.notes: use the same quantity

substitutions.substitute: sourdough, whole-wheat pav, or leftover chapatti — all make good bases
substitutions.notes: chapatti adds a pleasant chewiness

culturalNote: Soft-scrambled eggs on toast is the backbone of the British cafe breakfast and appears in almost every household. The key difference from the firmer diner-style scramble is the patient low-heat technique popularised by British chefs.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Any heavy-based non-stick pan or tawa works perfectly. Use Amul butter and Britannia or local thick-sliced bread. The low-heat method suits Indian gas burners well — keep the knob to minimum. A pinch of turmeric or a slit green chilli added with the eggs gives a desi twist without overwhelming the dish.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Ratatouille
description: A slow-cooked Provençal vegetable stew of brinjal, courgette, capsicum and tomato, perfumed with thyme and basil. The vegetables meld into a jammy, fragrant whole that is better the next day and delicious at any temperature.

ingredients.name: brinjal (eggplant), cut into 2 cm cubes
ingredients.notes: large, about 300 g

ingredients.name: red or yellow capsicum, deseeded and cut into strips
fresh basil leaves
ingredients.notes: stir in at the end for freshness

steps.text: Cut all the vegetables into pieces of roughly the same size so they cook evenly. Sprinkle the brinjal cubes with a pinch of salt, toss and leave in a colander for 10 minutes to draw out bitterness, then pat dry with a cloth.

Heat the olive oil in a wide, heavy kadai or pot over medium flame. Add the onion and garlic with a pinch of salt and sauté, stirring occasionally, until soft and translucent but not browned, about 8 minutes.
Add the capsicum strips and cook for 3 minutes, stirring once or twice, until they begin to soften at the edges.

Add the dried brinjal and zucchini to the pot and stir to coat everything in the oil. Let the vegetables cook together for 5 minutes, turning occasionally, so they pick up a little colour rather than steaming.

Tip in the chopped tomatoes along with the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. Season generously with salt and black pepper and stir to combine. Cover the pot, reduce to a low simmer and cook for 20 minutes, checking once and adding a splash of water if it looks dry.

Remove the lid and cook uncovered for a further 10–12 minutes until most of the liquid has evaporated and the vegetables are completely tender with a jammy, concentrated consistency. Discard the thyme stalks and bay leaves, tear in the basil leaves if using, and check the seasoning. Serve warm, at room temperature, or chilled — ratatouille improves as it sits.

substitutions.substitute: any neutral cooking oil — the flavour will be lighter but still pleasant
substitutions.notes: olive oil is preferable for authenticity

substitutions.substitute: ridge gourd (peerkangai) or bottle gourd (lauki) — same water content and neutral flavour
substitutions.notes: cut into similar-sized pieces

substitutions.substitute: fresh coriander leaves added at the end — gives a different but harmonious flavour
substitutions.notes: add just before serving

substitutions.substitute: dried thyme (half the quantity) or a pinch of dried oregano
substitutions.notes: dried herbs work fine in long-cooked dishes

culturalNote: Ratatouille originates in Provence, the sun-drenched south of France, and was originally peasant food — a thrifty way to cook a glut of summer vegetables. Its name likely derives from 'touiller', meaning to stir or toss.
regionalVariation: Some Provençal cooks cook each vegetable separately before combining, which gives cleaner flavour but doubles the effort. The version here, cooked together in one pot, is the weekday home approach.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A wide kadai is ideal — it gives good surface area for the vegetables to brown rather than steam. Olive oil is now widely available in Indian grocery stores (Borges and Del Monte are common brands), but any mild oil works. Zucchini is sold in many metro supermarkets; if unavailable, lauki (bottle gourd) is the closest substitute. The dish works beautifully with rice or chapatti.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: French Onion Soup
description: Deeply caramelised onions simmered in rich stock and ladled into bowls topped with a toast crouton and bubbling melted cheese. The 35-minute caramelisation is unhurried work that transforms sharp raw onion into something sweet and complex.

ingredients.name: large onions, thinly sliced into half-rings
vegetable stock (or beef stock for non-vegetarian)
thick bread slices (preferably sourdough or crusty white)
hard cheese, grated (gruyere, emmental, or Amul cheddar)
sugar
ingredients.notes: helps caramelisation in the final stage

steps.text: Melt the butter in a wide, heavy-bottomed pot or deep kadai over medium flame. Add all the sliced onions with a generous pinch of salt and stir to coat them in the butter. The onions will look like an enormous pile — they reduce dramatically.

Cook the onions over medium-low heat, stirring every 3–4 minutes, for 30–35 minutes until they are a deep amber-brown and soft throughout. Do not rush with high heat — the colour must come from true caramelisation, not burning. If the pot looks too dry, add a tablespoon of water and scrape the bottom.

Sprinkle in the sugar (if using) and cook 2 more minutes, stirring, to deepen the colour. Remove the bay leaves if you added them early. The onions are ready when they are sticky, deeply brown, and smell sweet.

Pour in the stock and add the bay leaves, thyme sprigs, and black pepper. Bring to a brisk boil then reduce to a gentle simmer. Cook uncovered for 15 minutes to let the flavours meld. Taste and adjust salt — the stock may already be salted.
Toast the bread slices until firm and golden on both sides — they need to be crisp enough to float rather than dissolve into the soup. Cut each slice to fit your serving bowl if needed.

Ladle the hot soup into oven-safe bowls, float a toast slice on each, and pile grated cheese on top. Place bowls under a grill or in a hot oven (220°C) for 3–5 minutes until the cheese is melted, golden, and beginning to bubble at the edges. Serve immediately while the cheese is still sizzling.

substitutions.substitute: Amul processed cheese slices (1–2 slices per bowl) — melt perfectly under indirect heat
substitutions.notes: or use any cheddar-style cheese

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp olive oil — gives a slightly less rich base but works well
substitutions.notes: the caramelisation takes the same time

substitutions.substitute: 1 litre water with 2 vegetable stock cubes (Maggi or Knorr)
substitutions.notes: good for budget cooking

substitutions.substitute: leftover dinner rolls or thick pav — toast in a dry kadai until crisp
substitutions.notes: shape is less important than crispness

culturalNote: French onion soup is rooted in Parisian market culture — vendors and night workers warmed themselves on it in the early hours at Les Halles market. The caramelised onion technique is the entire foundation of the dish.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A wide kadai or heavy-based pressure-cooker body works well for the long caramelisation stage. For the cheese topping without an oven, ladle the soup into heatproof steel bowls, float the toast, add grated Amul cheddar or processed cheese, then cover each bowl tightly with a lid or foil and rest for 3–4 minutes — the steam from the hot soup melts the cheese beautifully. Alternatively, briefly hold a hot tawa above the bowl to create radiant heat.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: French Sweet Crêpes
description: Thin, lacy French crêpes with a gentle sweetness and a touch of vanilla — perfect for filling with lemon and sugar, fruit jam, or chocolate spread. The batter needs 30 minutes to rest but the cooking is quick and satisfying.

ingredients.name: maida (all-purpose flour)
ingredients.notes: about 120 g; sift to remove lumps

ingredients.name: full-fat milk
ingredients.notes: at room temperature

ingredients.name: butter, melted and cooled
ingredients.notes: 1 tbsp in batter, 1 tbsp for greasing the pan

ingredients.name: lemon, for squeezing at the table

steps.text: Whisk together the flour, eggs, sugar and pinch of salt in a bowl until a thick paste forms. Gradually pour in the milk a little at a time, whisking constantly to avoid lumps, then stir in 1 tablespoon of the melted butter and the vanilla. The batter should be thin — about the consistency of single cream. Cover and rest for at least 30 minutes at room temperature.
Heat a 20–22 cm non-stick frying pan or tawa over medium heat. Brush or dab lightly with the remaining melted butter using a folded piece of cloth or kitchen paper — you need only a thin film.

Pour a small ladleful (about 60–70 ml) of batter into the centre of the hot pan and immediately tilt and swirl the pan in a circular motion so the batter spreads into a thin, even round. Cook for about 1–1.5 minutes until the edges look dry and the underside is pale gold.

Flip the crêpe with a thin spatula — or with a confident wrist flick if you're feeling brave — and cook the second side for 30–40 seconds. The second side will look spottier and less even than the first, which is normal. Slide onto a plate and repeat with remaining batter.

Stack the crêpes as you go — they stay warm and soft this way. Serve with a squeeze of lemon and a dusting of sugar, a spoon of jam, fresh banana slices, or any topping you like. Crêpes can be made ahead and reheated briefly in a dry pan.

substitutions.substitute: wheat flour (atta) for a slightly denser, more rustic crêpe — works well
substitutions.notes: the batter may need a splash more milk

substitutions.substitute: coconut milk for a dairy-free version with a subtle tropical note
substitutions.notes: thin it slightly with water if it seems too thick

substitutions.substitute: coconut oil (1 tbsp) — keeps the crêpes light and dairy-free
substitutions.notes: melt before using

substitutions.substitute: a pinch of cardamom powder — adds an Indian warmth that pairs beautifully with banana and honey
substitutions.notes: optional but recommended

culturalNote: Crêpes are associated with Brittany in north-western France, where thin buckwheat galettes and white-flour crêpes have been made for centuries. Crêpe Suzette — flambéed with orange butter — is the most famous formal version.
regionalVariation: Brittany's savoury galettes use buckwheat flour and have no sugar; they are a separate recipe. Sweet crêpes from the rest of France use plain white flour, as in this recipe.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A flat non-stick tawa (dosa tawa) is ideal and makes perfect crêpes. The thin-batter technique is virtually the same as making a dosa — the main difference is the tawa should not be as hot as for dosa; keep it medium rather than high. Vanilla essence (not extract) is the most widely available flavouring in Indian stores and works well here.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Savoury Crêpes with Mushroom and Cheese
description: Thin, golden crêpes filled with sautéed mushrooms, wilted spinach and melted cheese — the satisfying savoury counterpart to the classic French sweet crêpe. A quick weeknight dinner that looks far more elegant than the effort it requires.

ingredients.name: cold water
ingredients.notes: thins the batter to the right consistency

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.notes: 1 tbsp in batter, 1 tbsp for cooking

ingredients.name: fresh spinach
ingredients.notes: washed; about 100 g

steps.text: Whisk together the maida, egg, milk, cold water, a pinch of salt and 1 tablespoon of the melted butter in a bowl until perfectly smooth — no lumps. The batter should be thin, about the consistency of single cream. Cover and rest for 30 minutes at room temperature.

While the batter rests, melt the remaining butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Add the onion and cook 3 minutes until softened, then add the garlic and cook 1 minute more. Add the mushrooms and cook, stirring occasionally, for 5–6 minutes until their moisture has evaporated and they begin to brown.

Add the rinsed spinach to the mushrooms and stir until it wilts, about 2 minutes. Strip the leaves from the thyme sprigs if using and stir them in. Season the filling generously with salt and pepper and keep warm off the heat.

Heat a 22 cm non-stick pan or tawa over medium heat. Brush lightly with a little butter using a folded cloth. Pour in a small ladle of batter (about 60 ml) and swirl the pan immediately to form a thin, even round. Cook for about 1 minute until the edges lift and the underside is pale gold.

Flip the crêpe and cook the second side for 30 seconds. Place 2–3 tablespoons of the mushroom filling across one half of the crêpe and fold the crêpe in half over the filling. Scatter a tablespoon of grated cheese on top, cover the pan with a lid for 1 minute so the cheese melts, then slide onto a plate.
Repeat with the remaining batter and filling to make 8–10 crêpes in total. Serve immediately — savoury crêpes are best straight from the pan and do not hold well.

substitutions.substitute: capsicum, blanched broccoli, or corn kernels — any firm vegetable that holds up to sautéing
substitutions.notes: cook out the moisture fully before filling

substitutions.substitute: Amul processed cheese slices (1 per crêpe) — melt quickly and evenly
substitutions.notes: cheddar blocks also work well grated

substitutions.substitute: fenugreek leaves (methi) — wash well to remove bitterness; adds a pleasant depth
substitutions.notes: wilt briefly before using

substitutions.substitute: half maida and half wheat flour (atta) — gives a slightly more rustic, wholesome crêpe
substitutions.notes: may need a little extra milk

culturalNote: Savoury buckwheat galettes are the traditional street food of Brittany, sold at crêperies with endless filling combinations. The version here uses plain flour for accessibility while keeping the spirit of the galette complète.
regionalVariation: The classic Breton galette complète has egg, ham and cheese — crack an egg onto the crêpe in the pan, fold the edges inward to form a square, and let it set before serving.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A flat non-stick dosa tawa is ideal and makes the process feel familiar. Amul cheddar (block, grated) melts beautifully and is widely available. The mushroom filling can be enhanced with a pinch of garam masala or black pepper for a desi twist. Paneer can replace the cheese for a protein-rich alternative.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Crustless Mushroom and Cheese Quiche Bake
description: A silky baked egg custard studded with sautéed mushrooms, onions and melted cheese — all the richness of a French quiche without the pastry. This sets firmly in the oven and slices cleanly into wedges, making it ideal for a light lunch or a party spread.

ingredients.name: fresh spinach, roughly chopped
ingredients.notes: about 80 g; adds colour and nutrition

steps.text: Preheat your oven to 180°C. Grease a 22 cm ovenproof skillet or shallow baking dish generously with a little butter and set aside.

Melt the butter in a frying pan over medium heat. Sauté the onion for 3 minutes until soft, then add the garlic and cook 1 minute more. Add the mushrooms and cook for 5–6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they lose their moisture and turn golden. Add the spinach and stir until wilted, about 2 minutes. Season with salt, pepper and strip in the thyme leaves if using. Spread evenly in the prepared baking dish.

In a large bowl, whisk together the eggs, cream, milk, nutmeg, salt and pepper until fully combined and slightly frothy. Stir in half the grated cheese. Pour the egg mixture over the vegetables in the baking dish.

Scatter the remaining cheese evenly over the top. Place the dish in the preheated oven and bake for 30–35 minutes, until the top is golden and the custard is just set — a gentle wobble in the very centre is fine, as residual heat will finish it.
Remove from the oven and rest for 10 minutes before cutting. The quiche will settle as it cools and slices more cleanly. Scatter parsley over the top and serve warm or at room temperature.

substitutions.substitute: full-fat curd (hung briefly to remove whey) — gives a slightly tangier custard
substitutions.notes: use the same quantity; the texture will be a little denser

substitutions.substitute: broccoli florets (blanched), peas, or diced capsicum — use any combination of vegetables
substitutions.notes: make sure to cook out excess moisture before adding

substitutions.substitute: crumbled paneer (75 g) — does not melt the same way but gives a pleasant texture and protein
substitutions.notes: mix half into the custard and crumble rest on top

substitutions.substitute: fenugreek leaves (methi) — add a wonderful earthy bitterness; wilt briefly first
substitutions.notes: use about 50 g fresh methi leaves

culturalNote: The quiche originated in the Lorraine region of north-eastern France and was traditionally made with a lard pastry crust. The crustless version is a modern simplification that suits lighter eating and requires no pastry skills.
regionalVariation: The classic Quiche Lorraine has bacon (lardons) in place of mushrooms and uses no cheese in the original — the cheese-topped version is the modern French bistro adaptation.
indianKitchenAdaptation: If you have an oven, use it at 180°C exactly as written. Without an oven, use a pressure cooker as a steam oven: grease the inner vessel, pour in the egg mixture with the vegetables, cover with a plate (not the weight), and cook on low flame for 20–25 minutes until the custard is set — check at 20 minutes. The result is slightly denser but delicious. Amul cheddar block (grated) works perfectly as the cheese.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Potato Gratin (Gratin Dauphinois)
description: Thinly sliced potatoes baked slowly in cream and garlic until completely tender and yielding, with a bubbling golden cheese crust. Simple ingredients transformed by long, gentle heat into something far greater than the sum of their parts.

ingredients.name: starchy potatoes (Russet, Désirée, or any large potato)
garlic cloves
ingredients.notes: 2 for the cream, 1 for rubbing the dish

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.notes: for greasing the dish

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 160°C. Butter a shallow ovenproof baking dish (about 25×18 cm) generously, then cut one garlic clove in half and rub the entire interior of the dish with the cut surface. Discard that garlic piece.

Peel the potatoes and slice them very thin — about 2 mm — using a sharp knife or a mandoline. Do not rinse the slices; the surface starch helps the cream thicken and bind. Stack the slices as you go and keep them dry.

Slice the remaining garlic cloves finely. Combine the cream, milk, sliced garlic, thyme sprigs, nutmeg, salt and pepper in a small saucepan. Heat over medium until the mixture just begins to steam and small bubbles appear around the edge; do not boil. Remove from heat and set aside.

Arrange the potato slices in the prepared dish in overlapping layers like tiles, seasoning lightly with salt and pepper between each layer. Pour the warm cream mixture evenly over the potatoes — it should come about three-quarters up the stack.
Scatter the grated cheese evenly over the top. Cover the dish tightly with foil and place in the preheated oven. Bake covered for 40 minutes — the potatoes will cook through in the cream.

Remove the foil, increase the oven to 200°C and bake uncovered for a further 20 minutes until the top is deeply golden and bubbling and a knife or skewer slides through the potatoes with no resistance at all. Rest 10 minutes before cutting — this allows the cream to set slightly for neater portions.

substitutions.substitute: thick coconut milk (200 ml) + 100 ml milk — gives a dairy-free gratin with a subtle sweetness
substitutions.notes: reduce the milk quantity slightly; the flavour changes but the texture is similar

substitutions.substitute: Amul processed cheese slices (4–5 slices) laid on top — melt to a satisfying golden crust
substitutions.notes: or use Parmesan for a sharper flavour

substitutions.substitute: a bay leaf and a pinch of dried rosemary — equally aromatic in the cream infusion
substitutions.notes: remove the bay leaf before pouring

substitutions.substitute: thinly sliced raw banana (vazhakkai) for a starchier, more robust version — unusual but delicious
substitutions.notes: cooking time reduces to 50 minutes total

culturalNote: Gratin Dauphinois hails from the Dauphiné region near the French Alps, where the dish was traditionally cooked in the village baker's oven after the day's bread was done. The authentic version uses no cheese at all — just cream and garlic.
regionalVariation: The cheese-topped version is now more common in French bistros and home kitchens than the original. In Lyon, a version with onions is popular; in some parts of Provence, garlic is rubbed into the cream more aggressively for a sharper flavour.
indianKitchenAdaptation: If you have an oven, use it precisely as written — the slow low heat is what makes this dish. Without an oven, layer the potatoes in a kadai with a tight-fitting lid, pour the cream mixture over, cook on the lowest possible flame for 35–40 minutes until the potatoes are completely tender, then remove the lid and cook a further 5 minutes to concentrate. Finish by holding a hot tawa over the surface for 1–2 minutes to lightly brown the top. Fresh cream (Amul or similar) works perfectly here.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Chicken Chasseur
description: Bone-in chicken pieces browned until golden then braised with mushrooms, fresh tomatoes, herbs and stock into a rich, fragrant sauce. Chasseur means 'hunter' in French — this is the countryside braise that would have been made with game birds and whatever was foraged.

ingredients.name: chicken pieces, bone-in (thighs or mixed), skin-on
fresh parsley, finely chopped
ingredients.notes: for garnish

steps.text: Pat the chicken pieces completely dry with kitchen paper — moisture prevents browning. Season all sides generously with salt and pepper.

Heat the oil in a wide, heavy kadai or heavy-bottomed pan over high flame. Place the chicken pieces skin-side down and sear without moving for 5 minutes until the skin is deep golden. Flip and sear the other side for 3 minutes. Transfer the browned chicken to a plate.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the onion to the fat remaining in the pan and sauté for 4 minutes until translucent, scraping up any browned bits from the base. Add the garlic and cook 1 minute more.
Add the mushrooms and cook for 5–6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they release their liquid and the pan is nearly dry. They should take on a little colour at the edges.
Add the chopped tomatoes and cook for 3 minutes, pressing them with the back of a spoon until they collapse into the pan juices.

Return the browned chicken pieces to the pan, fitting them snugly. Pour in the stock and tuck in the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. Bring to a brisk simmer, then cover tightly and reduce the flame to medium-low.

Braise covered for 25 minutes, turning the chicken once halfway through, until the meat is cooked through and pulls away from the bone easily. If the sauce seems thin, uncover and simmer 5 more minutes to concentrate.

Remove the thyme stalks and bay leaves. Stir in the butter and taste for seasoning. Scatter the chopped parsley over the top and serve directly from the pan with steamed rice, mashed potato, or crusty bread.

substitutions.substitute: 500 g mutton or lamb pieces — increase braising time to 45 minutes
substitutions.notes: the sauce flavours work equally well with red meat

substitutions.substitute: ghee — adds a richer, nuttier finish
substitutions.notes: use the same quantity; stir in at the very end

substitutions.substitute: 250 ml water with a Knorr or Maggi vegetable stock cube dissolved in it
substitutions.notes: perfectly acceptable result

substitutions.substitute: 3 tbsp tomato paste dissolved in 150 ml water — gives a more concentrated, deeper flavour
substitutions.notes: reduce the stock by 50 ml if using paste

culturalNote: Poulet chasseur is one of the classic French braises — it appears in both the rustic farmhouse tradition and the refined bistro menu. The dish belongs to a family of 'chasseur' preparations that originally celebrated returning hunters with their catch.
regionalVariation: Some versions add a splash of cognac or white wine after browning the chicken and flame it — the alcohol burns off and adds depth. A Provençal variation adds black olives and capers to the sauce.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A kadai is ideal for this entire recipe — the shape encourages browning and the depth allows the braise. The technique (sear-then-braise) mirrors many Indian curries and feels natural on an Indian gas burner. Use desi chicken (country chicken) for the best flavour and texture; the longer connective tissue suits the braising method. A pressure cooker can be used: after step 6, cook under pressure for 2 whistles on medium, then reduce uncovered for 5 minutes.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Fish and Chips (Kadai Style)
description: Thick boneless fish fillets in a light, crisp beer-style batter alongside golden chunky chips — the great British pub classic made in a kadai. A double-fry on the chips and ice-cold batter on the fish are the two secrets to getting everything properly crisp.

ingredients.name: white fish fillets (tilapia, basa, pollock, or cod), boneless
large potatoes
ingredients.notes: about 700 g total

ingredients.name: maida (all-purpose flour)
ingredients.notes: for batter

ingredients.name: rice flour
ingredients.notes: adds extra crunch to the batter

ingredients.name: cold water
ingredients.notes: must be cold — warmth makes the batter dense

ingredients.name: lemon, cut into wedges for serving

steps.text: Peel the potatoes and cut into thick chips, about 2 cm wide and 7–8 cm long. Place in a large bowl of cold water and soak for 15 minutes to remove surface starch — this is what makes them crisp inside and fluffy within. Drain and pat completely dry with a cloth.

Make the batter: whisk together the maida, rice flour (if using), baking powder, black pepper and half a teaspoon of salt. Make a well in the centre, add the egg and pour in the cold water. Whisk until smooth — the batter should look like thick paint and coat the back of a spoon. Keep the batter cold; pop it in the fridge if the kitchen is warm.

Pour the oil into a deep kadai and heat over high flame. Test the temperature by dropping in a small chip — it should sizzle actively but not brown immediately (about 160–165°C). Add the chips in two batches and fry for 8–10 minutes, turning once or twice, until cooked through but still pale and soft. Drain on kitchen paper.

Increase the oil temperature until it shimmers and a drop of batter floats and sizzles immediately (about 180°C). Pat the fish fillets dry, season lightly with salt and pepper, then dip each fillet into the batter, letting the excess drip off for a second before lowering it carefully into the oil.

Fry the fish in batches (do not crowd the kadai) for 4–5 minutes per side until the batter is deep golden and the fish feels firm when pressed. Drain on kitchen paper and keep warm. Each fillet is done when the batter is crisp and doesn't deflect when tapped.

Return the blanched chips to the hot oil (which should be around 185°C now) and fry for 2–3 minutes until they are golden, crisp and irresistible. Work in batches to keep the oil hot. Drain, sprinkle with salt immediately.

Serve the fish and chips immediately — they do not improve with waiting. Arrange on a plate with lemon wedges on the side. Mint chutney or a squeeze of lime works beautifully as a dipping sauce in the Indian version.

substitutions.substitute: thick paneer slabs (400 g) for a vegetarian kadai fry — reduce frying time to 2–3 minutes
substitutions.notes: same batter and chip accompaniment work perfectly

substitutions.substitute: half maida and half rice flour (no additional rice flour needed) — gives a thinner, crispier batter
substitutions.notes: adjust water to achieve the same consistency

substitutions.substitute: 2 tbsp thick curd whisked into the batter — gives a slightly denser but still good coating
substitutions.notes: the batter may need 1 tbsp extra water

substitutions.substitute: raw banana (vazhakkai) cut into chips — par-boil 5 minutes then double-fry as described
substitutions.notes: holds up well and stays crispier for longer

culturalNote: Fish and chips became Britain's defining working-class street food in the industrial north of England in the mid-19th century — cheap, calorie-dense, and served in newspaper to keep the hands warm. Today it is the country's most beloved takeaway meal.
regionalVariation: Yorkshire shops traditionally fry in beef dripping for a deeper flavour; coastal Cornish chippies favour local haddock or pollock over cod. Mushy peas and pickled eggs are the classic northern accompaniments.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A deep kadai is the ideal Indian tool for this — wider than the traditional British chip pan but just as effective. Use basa or tilapia (widely available frozen in Indian cities), which are meaty, boneless fillets that hold up in hot oil. Add a pinch of red chilli powder or ajwain to the batter for a desi touch. Serve with mint-coriander chutney thinned with a little curd — it is a surprisingly natural match.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Shepherd's Pie
description: A deep, savoury minced meat filling packed with carrots, peas and herbs, topped with a thick layer of fluffy mashed potato and baked until the crust turns golden. One of the great British one-dish suppers, and genuinely improved by reheating the next day.

steps.text: Peel and roughly chop the potatoes. Cover with salted cold water in a saucepan and bring to a boil. Cook for 18–20 minutes until completely tender — a skewer should pass through with no resistance. Drain well, then mash with the butter, warm milk, salt and pepper until very smooth and fluffy. Set aside.

While the potatoes cook, heat a little oil in a wide kadai or heavy pan on high flame. Add the keema and cook, breaking it up with a spoon, for 6–8 minutes until well browned with no pink remaining. Good colour on the meat is what gives the filling depth.

Reduce the heat to medium. Push the browned keema to one side and add the onion, carrot and garlic to the cleared space. Sauté for 5 minutes until the vegetables begin to soften, then stir everything together.

Stir in the tomato paste and cook for 2 minutes until it darkens slightly. Pour in the stock and add the thyme and rosemary sprigs. Season with salt and pepper, bring to a simmer, cover and cook for 15 minutes until the carrots are tender and the sauce has reduced to a thick, scoopable consistency.
Stir in the peas, taste for seasoning, and discard the herb stalks. Preheat your oven to 200°C. Transfer the filling to a large baking dish (about 25×18 cm) and spread it into an even layer.

Spoon the mashed potato on top of the filling and spread it to cover completely, going right to the edges so no steam can escape. Use a fork to create ridges across the surface — these will brown and crisp beautifully in the oven.

Bake for 20–25 minutes until the mash top is golden-brown and the filling is bubbling at the edges. Rest 5 minutes before scooping and serving — the filling sets slightly and the portions hold together better.

substitutions.substitute: chickpeas (canned, 400 g, drained) or soya chunks (200 g, soaked) for a vegetarian version
substitutions.notes: mash the chickpeas lightly to help the filling bind

substitutions.substitute: ghee — makes the mash richer with a more pronounced flavour
substitutions.notes: use the same quantity; the mash will be slightly more fragrant

substitutions.substitute: coconut milk — gives a subtle sweetness and makes the dish dairy-free
substitutions.notes: use 0.25 cup as it is richer than dairy milk

substitutions.substitute: 2 ripe tomatoes, blended and cooked down — gives a fresher, slightly lighter flavour
substitutions.notes: cook for 5 minutes until the raw smell disappears

culturalNote: Shepherd's pie, made with lamb mince, is the older sibling of cottage pie (made with beef). Both dishes emerged in Britain as ways to use leftover roasted meat under a potato crust, and have been weekday staples since the 19th century.
regionalVariation: In Ireland, shepherd's pie is sometimes made with a colcannon (cabbage-mash) topping. Northern English versions often add Worcestershire sauce to the filling for a deeper umami kick.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Mutton keema is ideal and widely available in India — it suits the longer braising time and has excellent flavour. Use a pressure cooker for the filling: after browning the keema and adding the stock, cook for 2 whistles on medium, then open and reduce the sauce for 3 minutes. Ghee in the mash gives a richer result than butter. If you have no oven for the final step, spread the mash on top and cook covered on very low flame for 15 minutes, then remove the lid and increase heat for 2 minutes to form a light crust.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Chicken Tikka Masala (British Curry House Style)
description: Tender charred chicken tikka simmered in a velvety, mildly spiced tomato-cream sauce with a deeply orange-red colour — the curry house classic that Britain adopted as its own. Richer and creamier than its Indian relatives, this is the dish that put curry on the national menu.

ingredients.name: boneless chicken, cut into 4 cm cubes
Kashmiri red chilli powder (for colour and mild heat)

steps.text: Mix the curd, half the ginger-garlic paste, half the red chilli powder, half the cumin, half the coriander and turmeric with salt. Add the chicken cubes, coat well and marinate for at least 20 minutes at room temperature — or overnight in the fridge for the best flavour.

Heat a tawa or skillet on high flame until very hot. Arrange the marinated chicken pieces in a single layer (in batches if needed) and cook for 3–4 minutes without moving until charred at the edges. Flip and cook 2 more minutes until cooked through. The char is essential for the tikka flavour — don't skip this step. Set the cooked chicken aside.
In the same pan or a kadai, heat oil and butter together over medium flame. Add the onion and cook for 7–8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until deeply golden but not burnt.

Add the remaining ginger-garlic paste and cook for 2 minutes until the raw smell goes. Add the remaining red chilli powder, cumin, coriander and garam masala and fry the masala for 1 minute, stirring constantly.

Pour in the blended tomatoes, add salt and cook on medium-high, stirring regularly, for 10–12 minutes until the tomato raw smell has gone completely and the masala is thick and the oil begins to separate at the edges of the pan.
Pour in the cream and stir to combine. The sauce will turn a rich orange. Add 100 ml water if the sauce is too thick — it should coat the back of a spoon without being gluey.
Add the seared chicken pieces to the sauce. Simmer on low for 8–10 minutes so the chicken absorbs the flavour. Taste for salt, garnish with fresh coriander and serve with naan, rice or chapatti.

substitutions.substitute: cashew cream (soak 15 cashews in warm water 20 min, blend smooth with 100 ml water) — gives the same richness without dairy
substitutions.notes: a genuinely good dairy-free option

substitutions.substitute: paneer (400 g, cubed and pan-fried until golden) or firm tofu — reduce simmering time to 5 minutes
substitutions.notes: the sauce works beautifully with both

substitutions.substitute: thick coconut milk (100 ml) for the marinade — gives a different but still good result
substitutions.notes: the char will be slightly less vivid

substitutions.substitute: 200 ml canned crushed tomatoes or 3 tbsp tomato paste thinned with 150 ml water
substitutions.notes: paste gives a more concentrated, darker sauce

culturalNote: Chicken Tikka Masala is often cited as Britain's most popular dish, created in the 1970s by South Asian restaurateurs who added a mild tomato-cream sauce to grilled tikka to please British diners accustomed to sauce-heavy curries. Its precise origin is playfully disputed between Glasgow and London.
regionalVariation: The dish varies widely between curry houses — some make it very red with paprika and food colouring, others lean on cream for a pale orange. Balti versions are cooked and served in a small wok-like pan for a fresher, more smoky result.
indianKitchenAdaptation: This dish is inherently suited to an Indian kitchen — the two-step tikka-then-curry method is standard Indian cooking. Use Kashmiri red chilli powder for the vivid orange-red colour without excessive heat (it is the British curry house trick). A tawa works perfectly for the charring step; finish the sauce in a kadai. Amul fresh cream gives the authentic restaurant richness.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Coronation Chicken Salad
description: Poached chicken tossed in a golden, mildly curried dressing with sweet mango, plump raisins and toasted almonds — the iconic salad created for the coronation of Queen Elizabeth II in 1953. Serve stuffed into bread rolls, over rice, or piled on lettuce leaves.

ingredients.name: thick curd (hung 15 minutes to reduce whey), as a mayo substitute
ripe mango, peeled and finely diced
ingredients.notes: Alphonso or any sweet variety; or use 3 tbsp mango chutney

ingredients.name: celery stalks, thinly sliced
ingredients.notes: adds crunch; substitute cucumber if unavailable

ingredients.name: sugar
ingredients.notes: balances the tang of the curd

steps.text: Place the chicken in a saucepan and cover with cold water. Add a pinch of salt. Bring to a gentle boil, then reduce to a bare simmer and poach for 15–18 minutes until the chicken is cooked through with no pink in the centre — a thermometer should read 75°C at the thickest point. Lift out and cool for 10 minutes, then shred or cut into 2 cm pieces.

While the chicken cools, toast the flaked almonds in a dry frying pan over medium heat for 2–3 minutes, shaking occasionally, until golden and fragrant. Transfer immediately to a plate — they burn quickly once they start to colour.

Make the dressing: whisk together the hung curd, curry powder, lemon juice, sugar if using, salt and black pepper in a large bowl until smooth and creamy. Taste — the dressing should be gently spiced, slightly sweet and tangy.

Add the cooled chicken, diced mango, raisins and sliced celery to the dressing. Fold together gently until everything is evenly coated. If the dressing seems thick, stir in 1–2 tablespoons of cold water.

Scatter the toasted almonds and fresh coriander over the top just before serving. Coronation chicken is best served slightly chilled or at room temperature — not straight from the fridge. Serve in bread rolls, on buttered toast, over steamed basmati rice, or on a bed of soft lettuce leaves.

substitutions.substitute: equal parts hung curd and mayonnaise for a richer, more traditional dressing
substitutions.notes: the original recipe used mayo only; curd is the Indian substitute

substitutions.substitute: tinned peach slices (drained and diced) or fresh apple — both provide the sweet counterpoint
substitutions.notes: if using mango chutney, reduce the sugar to zero

substitutions.substitute: cashews (toasted) or sunflower seeds for a nut-free version
substitutions.notes: cashews are widely available and add a similar crunch

substitutions.substitute: cucumber (deseeded and diced) or spring onion — both add crunch without the assertive celery flavour
substitutions.notes: cucumber releases water, so add just before serving

culturalNote: Coronation Chicken was created by Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume for the banquet marking Queen Elizabeth II's coronation in June 1953. It was the first major British dish to embrace curry flavours, reflecting post-war Britain's growing familiarity with South Asian cooking.
regionalVariation: Modern versions range from the very mild (honey, mango chutney, no actual curry spice) to more robustly spiced interpretations. Some cafés add apricot jam for added sweetness; others fold in spinach or watercress.
indianKitchenAdaptation: Thick hung curd is the natural substitute for the mayonnaise in the original recipe — hang regular dahi in a muslin cloth for 15 minutes to reduce the water. Alphonso or Kesar mango is exceptional in this dish when in season; raw mango chutney (sweetened) works well out of season. The mild curry powder can be made at home with equal parts cumin, coriander, turmeric and a pinch of garam masala. Serve over jeera rice for a complete meal.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Mushroom and Thyme Toast
description: Golden sautéed mushrooms with fresh thyme and a touch of cream, piled onto thick toasted bread. A French bistro favourite that takes 15 minutes and delivers comfort far beyond its simplicity — an ideal quick supper or weekend brunch.

ingredients.name: mushrooms (button, cremini, or mixed), sliced or torn
fresh cream (for finishing)
ingredients.notes: adds richness; the dish is also lovely without it

ingredients.name: lemon
ingredients.notes: a small squeeze brightens the mushrooms

ingredients.name: fresh parsley, finely chopped
ingredients.notes: for garnish

steps.text: Toast the bread slices until deeply golden on both sides. You want substantial, sturdy toast that won't go soggy when the mushrooms are piled on. Set aside on plates while you cook the mushrooms.

Melt the butter in a wide frying pan or tawa over high heat until it foams. Add the garlic and let it sizzle for 20 seconds, then add the mushrooms in a single layer. Do not stir for the first minute — let them sear and take on colour. They need high heat and space, not steam.

After 1–2 minutes, once the mushrooms have browned on the base, toss them and cook for 3–4 more minutes until golden all over and any liquid in the pan has evaporated. The pan should look nearly dry — wet mushrooms on toast are a disappointment. Season generously with salt and pepper.

Reduce the heat to medium-low. Strip the thyme leaves from the stalks and add them to the pan. If using cream, pour it in now and stir for 30 seconds until it coats the mushrooms in a light sauce. Squeeze a little lemon juice over everything and toss.

Pile the mushrooms generously onto the toasted bread. Scatter chopped parsley over the top if using. Serve immediately — the contrast between the crisp hot toast and the savour mushrooms should be enjoyed straight away.

substitutions.substitute: olive oil for a lighter, dairy-free version — use 2 tbsp
substitutions.notes: the flavour will be less rich but the mushrooms will still brown well

substitutions.substitute: a spoonful of curd stirred in off the heat — adds a subtle tang rather than richness
substitutions.notes: do not boil the curd or it will split

substitutions.substitute: any wild or cultivated mushroom mix — shiitake, oyster and button together give excellent depth
substitutions.notes: tear rather than slice larger mushrooms for a better texture

substitutions.substitute: leftover chapatti or paratha, warmed and crisped on a dry tawa — the Indian adaptation works beautifully
substitutions.notes: pile on the mushrooms just before serving

culturalNote: Champignons sur toast appears in French bistros as a simple entrée or late-night snack, often served alongside a glass of wine. The combination of thyme, garlic and butter is the definitive French flavour palette for mushrooms.
regionalVariation: In Provence, a little fresh tomato or olive tapenade is sometimes added to the mushroom base. In the Burgundy tradition, a splash of red wine is deglazed into the pan after the mushrooms brown.
indianKitchenAdaptation: A non-stick tawa is ideal for getting the mushrooms golden without sticking. Amul butter browns beautifully and gives the right richness. Fresh cream (Amul) can be used in the finishing step. The dish is completely accessible with Indian pantry items. For an Indian twist, add a pinch of garam masala and replace parsley with coriander leaves — the result is a fusion mushroom toast that works brilliantly with pav.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen

title: Bread and Butter Pudding
description: Slices of buttered bread soaked in a spiced vanilla custard with plump raisins, baked until the top is golden and the inside is wobbly and custardy. The great British kitchen resourcefulness dish — stale bread becomes a dessert more satisfying than anything you could have bought.

ingredients.name: white bread, preferably a day old
sugar
ingredients.notes: 2 tbsp in the custard, 2 tbsp for the top

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 160°C. Grease a 20×25 cm baking dish with a little butter. Spread the remaining butter over one side of each bread slice. If the bread is very fresh, let the buttered slices air out for 10 minutes — slightly stale bread absorbs the custard more evenly.

Cut each buttered slice diagonally in half to make triangles. Arrange a single layer of bread in the baking dish (buttered side up), overlapping slightly. Scatter half the raisins and a pinch of cinnamon over this layer. Add the second layer of bread on top in the same overlapping pattern, buttered side up.

Whisk together the eggs, milk, cream, 2 tablespoons of sugar, vanilla, remaining cinnamon, nutmeg and salt until the sugar is dissolved and the mixture is fully combined. This is your custard — taste it; it should be pleasantly sweet and fragrant.

Pour the custard slowly and evenly over the bread, making sure every slice is well soaked. Gently press the top layer of bread down with the back of a spoon so it absorbs the liquid. Scatter the remaining raisins over the top. Let the dish rest for 10 minutes so the bread soaks fully before baking.

Sprinkle the remaining 2 tablespoons of sugar evenly over the surface. Place the dish in the preheated oven and bake for 35–40 minutes until the top is deep golden and slightly puffed, the custard is set around the edges but still has a gentle wobble in the centre.

Remove from the oven and rest for 10 minutes — the pudding will settle and the custard will firm up. Serve warm in scoops directly from the dish. It reheats well the next day with a splash of milk spooned over before warming.

substitutions.substitute: brioche, panettone, or leftover pav — enriched breads make the pudding more luxurious
substitutions.notes: reduce butter as these are already rich

substitutions.substitute: all milk (use 2 cups total) — lighter but still delicious
substitutions.notes: the custard will be slightly less set

substitutions.substitute: sultanas, diced dried apricot, or chocolate chips — all work well
substitutions.notes: soak dried fruit in warm water 15 minutes first for plumpness

substitutions.substitute: a pinch of cardamom powder (elaichi) — transforms this into a lovely Anglo-Indian dessert
substitutions.notes: reduce to 0.25 tsp as cardamom is assertive

culturalNote: Bread and butter pudding dates to at least the 18th century in Britain, rooted in the tradition of using stale bread economically. In wealthier households it used cream, spices and dried fruit — in poorer ones, just milk and sugar. Both were good.
regionalVariation: A classic Welsh variation uses bara brith (fruit bread) in place of white bread. Some modern British chefs make it with croissants for a richer, flakier result; others add marmalade between the bread layers for a bitter orange note.
indianKitchenAdaptation: White Britannia sandwich bread or local sliced bread works perfectly. Use Amul butter, Amul cream, and vanilla essence (widely available in Indian baking stores). Without an oven, use a pressure cooker as a steam oven: grease the inner vessel, pour in the assembled pudding, seal with a plate (do not use the weight), place the cooker over a tawa on very low flame, and steam for 25–30 minutes until set — the top won't brown but the custard will be creamy and delicious. Add cardamom to the custard for an irresistible Indian touch.
author: Cook Anything Kitchen
